steps.dialogue: Welcome back! You've been building fractions with bars, shading parts to [event:68fb9f66087679a0] show [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] [delay:0.5], [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction] [delay], [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4] three fourths [/fraction]. Today we're going to learn how these same fractions can live on a number line.

Remember this? [event:68fba6f99a48c636] One-half shown as a shaded bar. Watch what happens when we show the same fraction on a number line.[event:68fbaedeb6282d09][d:1.5] Same fraction, different view! The amount stays the same, we're just showing it differently.

Today we're going to discover what the [vocab]denominator[/vocab][event:69790fdc88b2891f] tells us when we work with number lines. Together, we'll learn how fractions  [event:69791076c875d5fd] describe parts [event:6979117d6fb528a6]of a whole and how they work to describe number lines.

This whole bar [event:6916012946b2a3b8] equals 1. You know how to split[event:6916018bea6f7ce6] the bar and shade to show [event:68fbb20f0b887bc7][fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction] of a bar.

metadata.notes: Change comparison animation to move top to bottom once supported

steps.dialogue: Let's look together. [event:68fbb87bf3b77df0] From 0 to 1 on the number line is also one whole [event:691603157eb65b40], just like the bar! To show [vocab]fourths[/vocab], we need four [event:68fbcb2f0944b9cb] equal spaces between 0 and 1.
Now it's your turn. Add a mark where you think one fourth will be on the number line.

prompt.text: Add a mark at one-fourth on the number line

step.dialogue: Let's try again. Remember you are adding a mark at one fourth.
Here's a hint: The bar has four [event:69161ab08470e61a] equal spaces. Use that as a guide to find one-fourth on the number line.

[event:69177e67f78b1d13] Follow along with me: just like the bar has [event:694317ca3c2da1db] four equal spaces, I [event:69433dc149efda04] divide the line into [event:6943186118a98da6] four equal spaces.  [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction] lines up with the [event:69161ce359951977] first partition.

on_correct.dialogue: Exactly. One-fourth of the way along the line!

steps.dialogue: See these spaces between the marks? They're just like the parts in our shaded bars, [event:69793c2ebd814c44]these spaces must be equal. Mathematicians call these spaces [vocab]intervals[/vocab], and [event:68fbcf1117a65e29]what matters is those spaces, or [vocab]intervals[/vocab], are [event:69793b159392f82a]all the same size!

Let's count the equal spaces together.[event:68fbd0642fca5a29] One, [d:0.3] two, [d:0.4] three, [d:0.4] four. Four equal spaces between 0 and 1 for [vocab]fourths[/vocab], just like four [event:69432647e7ecdfa5] equal parts in the bar!
So fractions aren't just parts of shapes, they're also positions on a number line! The [vocab]denominator[/vocab] tells us how many equal spaces on the line to create. Let's explore this more!
